# Build cross-device clipboard sync

> Hi codex, I I want the functionality of iPhone and MacBook clipboard. Like if I copy something on my iPhone, it automatically gets copied to my MacBook clipboard.
>
> And if I copy something on my MacBook, it gets automatically copied in my iPhone as well.
>
> Now the problem is I have an Android device, Android 12 to be specific, and a M3 MacBook Air.
>
> I have attached an image, look at it. like if I copy something on my Android device, it should automatically be copied on my MacBook as well. But in my MacBook I'm using a raycast. If I copy something on my MacBook, it should be copied on my Android phone as well.
>
> I know that solutions like KDE Connect exist but the problem is from my Android to MacBook I still have to click on send clipboard on my Android to be copied in MacBook.
>
> Plus a problem is it only works if both of my devices are connected on the same Wi-Fi.
>
> So in the image I have proposed a solution, is it possible to build something like that? I'm sure it would need we would need to build a Raycast extension for that as well as some Android client as well.
>
> And so to give you more context about the image, my solution is if I copy something on my Android phone that clipboard gets sent to a database in the cloud and that and from the database it gets seny to my raycast extension which will copy to my MacBook clipboard.
>
> And if I copy something on my MacBook, Raycast extension sends that to the cloud database and from the cloud database it automatically sends to my Android Device's ClipBoard.
>
> So is it possible to build something like that?
>
> User context
>
> Images:
>
> - `data:image/png;base64,`

<details><summary>2 previous messages</summary>

> I’m going to sanity-check the platform constraints before answering, because the make-or-break bits here are Android’s clipboard permissions and what Raycast can actually run in the background. Your diagram is architecturally sensible; the question is where the OSes put walls around it.
>
> <details><summary>Searched web 7 times</summary>
>

> - Searched web for `https://developers.raycast.com/information/lifecycle/background-refresh`
> - Searched web for `https://developer.android.com/preview/privacy/data-identifiers`
> - Searched web for `Android ClipboardManager setPrimaryClip background app allowed Android 12`
> - Searched web for `https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/touch-and-input/copy-paste`
> - Searched web for `'System notification shown when your app accesses clipboard data' in https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/touch-and-input/copy-paste`
>
> </details>
>
> </details>

</details>

Yes, your diagram is buildable, but not with perfect Apple Universal Clipboard behavior unless you accept one Android workaround.

The hard part is Android, not the cloud. Since Android 10+, a normal background app cannot freely read clipboard contents unless it is the focused app or the default keyboard/IME. Android 12 also shows a toast when an app reads clipboard data. Sources: [Android privacy clipboard restriction](https://developer.android.com/about/versions/10/privacy/changes#data-ids), [Android clipboard toast behavior](https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/touch-and-input/copy-paste#SystemNotification).

**What is feasible**

1. **MacBook to Android**
   - Raycast can read/write macOS clipboard using its Clipboard API.
   - A Raycast extension can push copied text to a cloud backend.
   - Android app can receive updates via WebSocket/FCM/Realtime DB.
   - Fully silent setting of Android clipboard from background may be flaky/restricted depending on Android/OEM behavior, so the reliable version may need a foreground service, notification action, or IME approach.

2. **Android to MacBook**
   - A normal Android app cannot just sit in the background and monitor every copy event.
   - To make it automatic, the Android client should probably be a **custom keyboard/IME**. Android allows the default IME to access clipboard data.
   - Alternative: use Accessibility Service, Shizuku, root, or ADB-style elevated access, but those are less clean, less Play-Store-friendly, and more fragile.

3. **Cloud sync**
   - Yes, cloud removes the same-Wi-Fi limitation.
   - Supabase or Firebase is a better fit than Cloudflare D1 because you want realtime updates.
   - Cloudflare could work, but you’d likely want Workers + Durable Objects/WebSockets, not only D1.

**Raycast caveat**

Raycast supports clipboard read/write: [Raycast Clipboard API](https://developers.raycast.com/api-reference/clipboard). It also supports background refresh, but scheduled background commands are interval-based, minimum `10s`, and not exact: [Raycast Background Refresh](https://developers.raycast.com/information/lifecycle/background-refresh). So a pure Raycast extension may be “near realtime,” not instant.

For best results, I’d build it like this:

```text
Android IME app
  -> encrypt clipboard locally
  -> Supabase/Firebase realtime channel
  -> Raycast extension or small macOS daemon
  -> macOS clipboard

macOS clipboard watcher
  -> encrypt locally
  -> Supabase/Firebase realtime channel
  -> Android IME / foreground service
  -> Android clipboard
```

Important details: end-to-end encryption, device IDs to prevent sync loops, deduplication hashes, TTL/auto-delete for clips, and “pause sync” controls for passwords or sensitive data.

So: **yes, possible as a personal tool or side-loaded app; not possible as a normal Android app with perfect invisible background clipboard monitoring.** The most realistic clean version is Android custom keyboard + cloud realtime backend + Raycast/macOS companion.

> Yes, I actually want to build this as a personal tool.
>
> And you know what? I want you to start building this. And tell me what all are you going to build?
>
> Create a folder in the projects folder, name it RayClip and start building. And also tell me how to run that.

<details><summary>18 previous messages</summary>

> Great, let’s make it real. I’m going to build this as a personal-tool prototype with three pieces: a realtime cloud API, a macOS clipboard bridge suitable for Raycast/manual use, and an Android client skeleton that uses the IME route for the OS clipboard restrictions.
>
> I found `~/projects`, and there isn’t a `RayClip` folder there yet. Because this folder sits outside the writable sandbox, I’ll draft the project here first using normal patch edits, then ask for permission to place the finished folder into `~/projects/RayClip`.
>
> The MVP shape I’m committing to: a tiny authenticated sync server with SSE events, a continuous macOS agent for real realtime behavior, a Raycast extension for clipboard commands/background polling, and an Android app skeleton that becomes your default keyboard so Android 12 allows clipboard access. That gives us a useful personal prototype without pretending Android’s privacy wall is not there.
>

>
> Raycast wants a real PNG icon in `assets`, so I’m generating a tiny custom 512px icon asset too. It’s not the soul of the app, but missing icons can make `ray develop` grumpy.
>
> <details><summary>Edited 2 files, called 1 tool</summary>
>
> MCP tool call
>
> node_repl.js
>
> ```json
> {
>   "title": "Generate Raycast icon",
>   "code": "const fs = await import('node:fs');\nconst path = await import('node:path');\nconst zlib = await import('node:zlib');\nconst outDir = path.resolve(nodeRepl.cwd, 'RayClip/raycast-extension/assets');\nfs.mkdirSync(outDir, { recursive: true });\nconst width = 512;\nconst height = 512;\nconst raw = Buffer.alloc((width * 4 + 1) * height);\nfunction setPixel(row, x, r, g, b, a = 255) {\n  const idx = row * (width * 4 + 1) + 1 + x * 4;\n  raw[idx] = r; raw[idx + 1] = g; raw[idx + 2] = b; raw[idx + 3] = a;\n}\nfunction inRoundedRect(x, y, left, top, right, bottom, radius) {\n  if (x < left || x > right || y < top || y > bottom) return false;\n  const cx = x < left + radius ? left + radius : x > right - radius ? right - radius : x;\n  const cy = y < top + radius ? top + radius : y > bottom - radius ? bottom - radius : y;\n  const dx = x - cx;\n  const dy = y - cy;\n  return dx * dx + dy * dy <= radius * radius;\n}\nfor (let y = 0; y < height; y++) {\n  raw[y * (width * 4 + 1)] = 0;\n  for (let x = 0; x < width; x++) {\n    const bg = 30 + Math.round((x / width) * 20);\n    const blue = 130 + Math.round((y / height) * 70);\n    setPixel(y, x, 20, bg, blue);\n    const board = inRoundedRect(x, y, 136, 126, 376, 410, 34);\n    const paper = inRoundedRect(x, y, 164, 178, 348, 370, 20);\n    const clip = inRoundedRect(x, y, 204, 92, 308, 166, 26);\n    const clipHole = inRoundedRect(x, y, 228, 112, 284, 148, 16);\n    if (board) setPixel(y, x, 239, 246, 255);\n    if (paper) setPixel(y, x, 37, 99, 235);\n    if (clip && !clipHole) setPixel(y, x, 239, 246, 255);\n    const line1 = y >= 224 && y <= 238 && x >= 198 && x <= 314;\n    const line2 = y >= 270 && y <= 284 && x >= 198 && x <= 314;\n    const line3 = y >= 316 && y <= 330 && x >= 198 && x <= 286;\n    if (line1 || line2 || line3) setPixel(y, x, 239, 246, 255);\n  }\n}\nfunction crcTable() {\n  const table = new Uint32Array(256);\n  for (let n = 0; n < 256; n++) {\n    let c = n;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 8; k++) c = (c & 1) ? (0xedb88320 ^ (c >>> 1)) : (c >>> 1);\n    table[n] = c >>> 0;\n  }\n  return table;\n}\nconst table = crcTable();\nfunction crc32(buf) {\n  let c = 0xffffffff;\n  for (const byte of buf) c = table[(c ^ byte) & 0xff] ^ (c >>> 8);\n  return (c ^ 0xffffffff) >>> 0;\n}\nfunction chunk(type, data) {\n  const typeBuf = Buffer.from(type);\n  const len = Buffer.alloc(4);\n  len.writeUInt32BE(data.length, 0);\n  const crc = Buffer.alloc(4);\n  crc.writeUInt32BE(crc32(Buffer.concat([typeBuf, data])), 0);\n  return Buffer.concat([len, typeBuf, data, crc]);\n}\nconst signature = Buffer.from([137, 80, 78, 71, 13, 10, 26, 10]);\nconst ihdr = Buffer.alloc(13);\nihdr.writeUInt32BE(width, 0);\nihdr.writeUInt32BE(height, 4);\nihdr[8] = 8;\nihdr[9] = 6;\nihdr[10] = 0;\nihdr[11] = 0;\nihdr[12] = 0;\nconst png = Buffer.concat([signature, chunk('IHDR', ihdr), chunk('IDAT', zlib.deflateSync(raw)), chunk('IEND', Buffer.alloc(0))]);\nfs.writeFileSync(path.join(outDir, 'icon.png'), png);\nnodeRepl.write(path.join(outDir, 'icon.png'));"
> }
> ```
>

> The server API is verified now: wrong token returns `401`, valid upload returns `201`, and latest pull returns the uploaded clip. I’m ready to place the finished folder into your actual `~/projects/RayClip` location.

</details>

Built the first RayClip MVP in [~/projects/RayClip](~/projects/RayClip).

What’s inside:

- [server](~/projects/RayClip/server/src/server.js): authenticated Node sync API with `/v1/clips`, `/v1/clips/latest`, and SSE `/v1/events`.
- [mac-agent](~/projects/RayClip/mac-agent/src/agent.js): continuous macOS clipboard bridge using `pbpaste`/`pbcopy`.
- [raycast-extension](~/projects/RayClip/raycast-extension/src/rayclip.ts): Raycast push, pull, and 10-second background sync commands.
- [android](~/projects/RayClip/android): Android app skeleton with settings screen, foreground sync service, and IME service for Android clipboard access.

How to run:

```sh
cd ~/projects/RayClip/server
cp .env.example .env
# set RAYCLIP_TOKEN in .env
npm start
```

Then in another terminal:

```sh
cd ~/projects/RayClip/mac-agent
cp .env.example .env
# set RAYCLIP_API_URL and RAYCLIP_TOKEN
npm start
```

For Raycast:

```sh
cd ~/projects/RayClip/raycast-extension
npm install
npm run dev
```

For Android, open [android](~/projects/RayClip/android) in Android Studio, install the app, enter the same server URL/token, start the sync service, then enable/select RayClip as the keyboard.

Verified locally: server syntax check, mac-agent syntax check, API upload/pull, and wrong-token `401` behavior. I couldn’t build Android here because `gradle` isn’t installed in this shell, and I couldn’t build Raycast because the `ray` CLI isn’t installed yet.

One important truth-in-labeling bit: the Android keyboard is currently a proof-of-path IME, not a usable Gboard replacement. That’s the correct technical route per Android’s clipboard restrictions, but the next step is making that Android side actually pleasant to use. Sources I leaned on: [Android clipboard docs](https://developer.android.com/develop/ui/views/touch-and-input/copy-paste), [Raycast manifest docs](https://developers.raycast.com/information/manifest), and [Raycast background refresh docs](https://developers.raycast.com/information/lifecycle/background-refresh).
